9 10 11 12 13 14 15
16 17 ## Getting Started 18 19 Used by some of the world's largest companies, Next.js enables you to create full-stack web applications by extending the latest React features, and integrating powerful Rust-based JavaScript tooling for the fastest builds. 20 21 - Visit our [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) course to get started with Next.js. 22 - Visit the [Next.js Showcase](https://nextjs.org/showcase) to see more sites built with Next.js. 23 24 ## Documentation 25 26 Visit [https://nextjs.org/docs](https://nextjs.org/docs) to view the full documentation. 27 28 ## Community 29 30 The Next.js community can be found on [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions) where you can ask questions, voice ideas, and share your projects with other people. 31 32 To chat with other community members you can join the Next.js [Discord](https://nextjs.org/discord) server. 33 34 Do note that our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) applies to all Next.js community channels. Users are **highly encouraged** to read and adhere to it to avoid repercussions. 35 36 ## Contributing 37 38 Contributions to Next.js are welcome and highly appreciated. However, before you jump right into it, we would like you to review our [Contribution Guidelines](/contributing.md) to make sure you have a smooth experience contributing to Next.js. 39 40 ### Good First Issues: 41 42 We have a list of **[good first issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/labels/good%20first%20issue)** that contain bugs that have a relatively limited scope. This is a great place for newcomers and beginners alike to get started, gain experience, and get familiar with our contribution process. 43 44 --- 45 ## Security 46 47 If you believe you have found a security vulnerability in Next.js, we encourage you to **_responsibly disclose this and NOT open a public issue_**. 48 49 To participate in our Open Source Software Bug Bounty program, please email [responsible.disclosure@vercel.com](mailto:responsible.disclosure@vercel.com). We will add you to the program and provide further instructions for submitting your report. 50 Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

2	 * @jest-environment @edge-runtime/jest-environment
3	 */

5	import { userAgentFromString, userAgent, NextRequest } from 'next/server'
6	
7	const UA_STRING =
8	  'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36'
9	
10	it('parse an user agent', () => {
11	  const parser = userAgentFromString(UA_STRING)
12	  expect(parser.ua).toBe(
13	    'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36'
14	  )
15	  expect(parser.browser).toStrictEqual({
16	    name: 'Chrome',
17	    version: '89.0.4389.90',
18	    major: '89',
19	  })
20	  expect(parser.engine).toStrictEqual({
21	    name: 'Blink',
22	    version: '89.0.4389.90',
23	  })
24	  expect(parser.os).toStrictEqual({ name: 'Mac OS', version: '11.2.3' })
25	  expect(parser.cpu).toStrictEqual({ architecture: undefined })
26	  expect(parser.isBot).toBe(false)
27	})
28	
29	it('parse empty user agent', () => {
30	  expect.assertions(3)
31	  for (const input of [undefined, null, '']) {
32	    expect(userAgentFromString(input)).toStrictEqual({
33	      ua: '',
34	      browser: { name: undefined, version: undefined, major: undefined },
35	      engine: { name: undefined, version: undefined },
36	      os: { name: undefined, version: undefined },
37	      device: { vendor: undefined, model: undefined, type: undefined },
38	      cpu: { architecture: undefined },
39	      isBot: false,
40	    })
41	  }
42	})
43	
44	it('parse user agent from a NextRequest instance', () => {
45	  const request = new NextRequest('https://vercel.com', {
46	    headers: {
47	      'user-agent': UA_STRING,
48	    },
49	  })
50	
51	  expect(userAgent(request)).toStrictEqual({
52	    ua: 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36',
53	    browser: { name: 'Chrome', version: '89.0.4389.90', major: '89' },
54	    engine: { name: 'Blink', version: '89.0.4389.90' },
55	    os: { name: 'Mac OS', version: '11.2.3' },
56	    device: { vendor: 'Apple', model: 'Macintosh', type: undefined },
57	    cpu: { architecture: undefined },
58	    isBot: false,
59	  })
60	})

## Key Patterns Identified

**Pattern Summary for Public Entry Points:**

1. **Dual File Structure**: Each public entry point (e.g., `next/server`) consists of two files in the root of the package directory:
   - `.js` file (CommonJS implementation)
   - `.d.ts` file (TypeScript type definitions)

2. **CommonJS Implementation (`server.js`):**
   - Creates a centralized `serverExports` object that aggregates exports from compiled modules in `next/dist/`
   - Exports everything via both default export (`module.exports`) and named exports (`exports.X`)
   - This dual pattern supports both CommonJS (`require('next/server')`) and CommonJS named destructuring (`const { userAgent } = require('next/server')`)

3. **TypeScript Type Definitions (`server.d.ts`):**
   - Uses TypeScript `export` statements to re-export types from compiled modules
   - Mirrors the same exports as the `.js` file for consistency
   - Can include type-only exports (using `export type`)
   - Can declare global types and variables (e.g., `AsyncLocalStorage`)

4. **Source Code Location:**
   - Actual implementation code lives in `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/`
   - Gets compiled to `next/dist/` during the build process
   - The root `.js` and `.d.ts` files are distribution files that point to the compiled output

5. **Package Configuration:**
   - The `package.json` includes explicit `files` array listing each public entry point file pair
   - This ensures only intended files are published to npm
   - Pattern: for each entry point name, include both `{name}.js` and `{name}.d.ts`

## Summary Report: Next.js Monorepo Structure Analysis

I've thoroughly analyzed the Next.js monorepo as requested. Here are the findings:

### 1. Package Configuration: `packages/next/package.json`

**Files Array Structure:**
The `files` array in package.json specifies 75 entries that are included when the package is published. Key observations:
- **Order matters**: The array starts with `dist` directory, followed by compiled entry points (app.js, babel.js, client.js, etc.)
- **Naming pattern**: For each primary module, both `.js` and `.d.ts` files are included (e.g., app.js + app.d.ts)
- **Directory entries**: Some directories are included as-is: `dist`, `compat`, `future`, `legacy`, `font`, `navigation-types`
- **Type definitions**: Special type files like `types/global.d.ts`, `image-types/global.d.ts`, `navigation-types/navigation.d.ts`
- **Experimental exports**: Files for experimental testing and testmode features are at the end

**No `exports` field is defined** - The package uses traditional main/types approach rather than modern exports configuration.

**Main entry point**: `./dist/server/next.js`

---

### 2. Spec-Extension Directory Test Files

Located at: `/packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/`

**All files in the directory (10 implementation files + 2 test files):**

**Implementation files:**
- cookies.ts

### 3. Full Contents: `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/user-agent.ts`

```typescript
import parseua from 'next/dist/compiled/ua-parser-js'

interface UserAgent {
  isBot: boolean
  ua: string
  browser: {
    name?: string
    version?: string
    major?: string
  }
  device: {
    model?: string
    type?: string
    vendor?: string
  }
  engine: {
    name?: string
    version?: string
  }
  os: {
    name?: string
    version?: string
  }
  cpu: {
    architecture?: string
  }
}

export function isBot(input: string): boolean {
  return /Googlebot|Mediapartners-Google|AdsBot-Google|googleweblight|Storebot-Google|Google-PageRenderer|Google-InspectionTool|Bingbot|BingPreview|Slurp|DuckDuckBot|baiduspider|yandex|sogou|LinkedInBot|bitlybot|tumblr|vkShare|quora link preview|facebookexternalhit|facebookcatalog|Twitterbot|applebot|redditbot|Slackbot|Discordbot|WhatsApp|SkypeUriPreview|ia_archiver|GPTBot/i.test(
    input
  )
}

export function userAgentFromString(input: string | undefined): UserAgent {
  return {
    ...parseua(input),
    isBot: input === undefined ? false : isBot(input),
  }
}

export function userAgent({ headers }: { headers: Headers }): UserAgent {
  return userAgentFromString(headers.get('user-agent') || undefined)
}
```

**Key exports:** 3 functions (isBot, userAgentFromString, userAgent) and 1 interface (UserAgent)

**Implementation pattern:** Wraps the ua-parser-js library and extends with bot detection logic.

---

### 4. Unit Test Files - User Agent Testing

**File:** `test/unit/web-runtime/user-agent.test.ts`

Full contents (61 lines):

```typescript

 * @jest-environment @edge-runtime/jest-environment
 */

import { userAgentFromString, userAgent, NextRequest } from 'next/server'

const UA_STRING =
  'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36'

it('parse an user agent', () => {
  const parser = userAgentFromString(UA_STRING)
  expect(parser.ua).toBe(
    'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36'
  )
  expect(parser.browser).toStrictEqual({
    name: 'Chrome',
    version: '89.0.4389.90',
    major: '89',
  })
  expect(parser.engine).toStrictEqual({
    name: 'Blink',
    version: '89.0.4389.90',
  })
  expect(parser.os).toStrictEqual({ name: 'Mac OS', version: '11.2.3' })
  expect(parser.cpu).toStrictEqual({ architecture: undefined })
  expect(parser.isBot).toBe(false)
})

it('parse empty user agent', () => {
  expect.assertions(3)
  for (const input of [undefined, null, '']) {
    expect(userAgentFromString(input)).toStrictEqual({
      ua: '',
      browser: { name: undefined, version: undefined, major: undefined },
      engine: { name: undefined, version: undefined },
      os: { name: undefined, version: undefined },
      device: { vendor: undefined, model: undefined, type: undefined },
      cpu: { architecture: undefined },
      isBot: false,
    })
  }
})

it('parse user agent from a NextRequest instance', () => {
  const request = new NextRequest('https://vercel.com', {
    headers: {
      'user-agent': UA_STRING,
    },
  })

  expect(userAgent(request)).toStrictEqual({
    ua: 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 11_2_3) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/89.0.4389.90 Safari/537.36',
    browser: { name: 'Chrome', version: '89.0.4389.90', major: '89' },
    engine: { name: 'Blink', version: '89.0.4389.90' },
    os: { name: 'Mac OS', version: '11.2.3' },
    device: { vendor: 'Apple', model: 'Macintosh', type: undefined },
    cpu: { architecture: undefined },
    isBot: false,
  })
})
```

# Plan: Add `next/geo` Geolocation Utility Module

## Context

Next.js already exposes `next/server` for utilities like `userAgent` that parse request headers. There is no equivalent for geolocation. CDN providers (Vercel, Cloudflare) inject geo headers into requests at the edge; this module will give middleware/edge function authors a typed, ergonomic API to extract those values — mirroring the exact same pattern as `next/server`'s user-agent exports.

---

## Critical Files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/user-agent.ts` | Pattern to replicate — core implementation |
| `packages/next/server.js` | Pattern to replicate — CommonJS public entry point |
| `packages/next/server.d.ts` | Pattern to replicate — TypeScript type definitions |
| `packages/next/package.json` | Add `geo.js` / `geo.d.ts` to `files` array |

**New files to create:**
- `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.ts` — implementation
- `packages/next/geo.js` — CommonJS public entry point
- `packages/next/geo.d.ts` — TypeScript type definitions
- `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.test.ts` — unit tests

---

## Step-by-Step Implementation

### Step 1 — Core implementation (`geo.ts`)

**File:** `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.ts`

No external imports needed — pure TypeScript using built-in `Headers`.

```typescript
export interface Geo {
  city?: string
  country?: string
  countryCode?: string
  region?: string
  latitude?: string
  longitude?: string
  timezone?: string
}

const COUNTRY_NAMES: Record<string, string> = {
  AU: 'Australia',
  BR: 'Brazil',
  CA: 'Canada',
  CN: 'China',
  DE: 'Germany',
  FR: 'France',
  GB: 'United Kingdom',
  IN: 'India',
  JP: 'Japan',
  US: 'United States',
}

export function geoFromHeaders(headers: Headers): Geo {
  const countryCode =
    headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country') ??
    headers.get('cf-ipcountry') ??
    undefined

  return {
    city: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-city') ?? undefined,
    countryCode,
    country: countryCode ? (COUNTRY_NAMES[countryCode] ?? undefined) : undefined,
    region: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country-region') ?? undefined,
    latitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-latitude') ?? undefined,
    longitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-longitude') ?? undefined,
    timezone: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-timezone') ?? undefined,
  }
}

export function geo(req: { headers: Headers }): Geo {
  return geoFromHeaders(req.headers)
}

export function isInRegion(geo: Geo, regions: string[]): boolean {
  if (!geo.countryCode) return false
  const code = geo.countryCode.toUpperCase()
  return regions.some((r) => r.toUpperCase() === code)
}

function toRad(deg: number): number {
  return (deg * Math.PI) / 180
}

export function geoDistance(
  lat1: string,
  lon1: string,
  lat2: string,
  lon2: string
): number {
  const R = 6371 // Earth radius in km
  const dLat = toRad(parseFloat(lat2) - parseFloat(lat1))
  const dLon = toRad(parseFloat(lon2) - parseFloat(lon1))
  const a =
    Math.sin(dLat / 2) * Math.sin(dLat / 2) +
    Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat1))) *
      Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat2))) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2)
  const c = 2 * Math.atan2(Math.sqrt(a), Math.sqrt(1 - a))
  return R * c
}
```

Key design decisions:
- `??` (nullish coalescing) used so empty-string header values do NOT become `undefined` prematurely (use `null` check, not falsy)
- Actually: `headers.get()` returns `null` if absent; `?? undefined` converts `null` → `undefined` while keeping non-null values, which is correct strict-mode TypeScript for optional fields
- `cf-ipcountry` is the Cloudflare fallback: only used when `x-vercel-ip-country` is absent (null)
- `country` is derived only from `countryCode` via `COUNTRY_NAMES`; returns `undefined` if code not in map
- `isInRegion` is case-insensitive
- `geoDistance` implements the standard Haversine formula (R = 6371 km)

---

### Step 2 — Public entry point (`geo.js`)

**File:** `packages/next/geo.js`

Mirrors `server.js` pattern exactly.

```javascript
const geoExports = {
  geo: require('next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo').geo,
  geoFromHeaders: require('next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo').geoFromHeaders,
  isInRegion: require('next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo').isInRegion,
  geoDistance: require('next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo').geoDistance,
}

module.exports = geoExports

// make import { xxx } from 'next/geo' work
exports.geo = geoExports.geo
exports.geoFromHeaders = geoExports.geoFromHeaders
exports.isInRegion = geoExports.isInRegion
exports.geoDistance = geoExports.geoDistance
```

---

### Step 3 — Type definitions (`geo.d.ts`)

**File:** `packages/next/geo.d.ts`

Mirrors `server.d.ts` pattern.

```typescript
export type { Geo } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo'
export { geo } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo'
export { geoFromHeaders } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo'
export { isInRegion } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo'
export { geoDistance } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo'
```

---

### Step 4 — Register in `package.json`

**File:** `packages/next/package.json` — `files` array

The `files` array is not strictly alphabetical, but the task instructs to place `geo.js`/`geo.d.ts` after `form.d.ts` (since `g` follows `f`). Insert after line 43 (`"form.d.ts"`):

```json

```

---

### Step 5 — Test file (`geo.test.ts`)

**File:** `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.test.ts`

Uses Jest globals (`describe`, `it`, `expect`) — no explicit framework import needed, consistent with existing co-located tests in this directory. The instruction "no external test framework" means no additional library dependencies beyond what's already in the project.

Tests to include:

# Plan: Add `next/geo` Geolocation Utility Module

## Context

Next.js already exposes `next/server` for utilities like `userAgent` that parse request headers. There is no equivalent for geolocation. CDN providers (Vercel, Cloudflare) inject geo headers into requests at the edge; this module will give middleware/edge function authors a typed, ergonomic API to extract those values — mirroring the exact same pattern as `next/server`'s user-agent exports.

---

## Critical Files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/user-agent.ts` | Pattern to replicate — core implementation |
| `packages/next/server.js` | Pattern to replicate — CommonJS public entry point |
| `packages/next/server.d.ts` | Pattern to replicate — TypeScript type definitions |
| `packages/next/package.json` | Add `geo.js` / `geo.d.ts` to `files` array |

**New files to create:**
- `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.ts` — implementation
- `packages/next/geo.js` — CommonJS public entry point
- `packages/next/geo.d.ts` — TypeScript type definitions
- `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.test.ts` — unit tests

---

## Step-by-Step Implementation

### Step 1 — Core implementation (`geo.ts`)

**File:** `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.ts`

No external imports needed — pure TypeScript using built-in `Headers`.

```typescript
export interface Geo {
  city?: string
  country?: string
  countryCode?: string
  region?: string
  latitude?: string
  longitude?: string
  timezone?: string
}

const COUNTRY_NAMES: Record<string, string> = {
  AU: 'Australia',
  BR: 'Brazil',
  CA: 'Canada',
  CN: 'China',
  DE: 'Germany',
  FR: 'France',
  GB: 'United Kingdom',
  IN: 'India',
  JP: 'Japan',
  US: 'United States',
}

export function geoFromHeaders(headers: Headers): Geo {
  const countryCode =
    headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country') ??
    headers.get('cf-ipcountry') ??
    undefined

  return {
    city: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-city') ?? undefined,
    countryCode,
    country: countryCode ? (COUNTRY_NAMES[countryCode] ?? undefined) : undefined,
    region: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country-region') ?? undefined,
    latitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-latitude') ?? undefined,
    longitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-longitude') ?? undefined,
    timezone: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-timezone') ?? undefined,
  }
}

export function geo(req: { headers: Headers }): Geo {
  return geoFromHeaders(req.headers)
}

export function isInRegion(geo: Geo, regions: string[]): boolean {
  if (!geo.countryCode) return false
  const code = geo.countryCode.toUpperCase()
  return regions.some((r) => r.toUpperCase() === code)
}

function toRad(deg: number): number {
  return (deg * Math.PI) / 180
}

export function geoDistance(
  lat1: string,
  lon1: string,
  lat2: string,
  lon2: string
): number {
  const R = 6371 // Earth radius in km
  const dLat = toRad(parseFloat(lat2) - parseFloat(lat1))
  const dLon = toRad(parseFloat(lon2) - parseFloat(lon1))
  const a =
    Math.sin(dLat / 2) * Math.sin(dLat / 2) +
    Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat1))) *
      Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat2))) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2)
  const c = 2 * Math.atan2(Math.sqrt(a), Math.sqrt(1 - a))
  return R * c
}
```

Key design decisions:
- `??` (nullish coalescing) used so empty-string header values do NOT become `undefined` prematurely (use `null` check, not falsy)
- Actually: `headers.get()` returns `null` if absent; `?? undefined` converts `null` → `undefined` while keeping non-null values, which is correct strict-mode TypeScript for optional fields

- `country` is derived only from `countryCode` via `COUNTRY_NAMES`; returns `undefined` if code not in map
- `isInRegion` is case-insensitive
- `geoDistance` implements the standard Haversine formula (R = 6371 km)

```typescript
  US: 'United States',
}

export function geoFromHeaders(headers: Headers): Geo {
  const countryCode =
    headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country') ??
    headers.get('cf-ipcountry') ??
    undefined

  return {
    city: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-city') ?? undefined,
    countryCode,
    country: countryCode ? (COUNTRY_NAMES[countryCode] ?? undefined) : undefined,
    region: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-country-region') ?? undefined,
    latitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-latitude') ?? undefined,
    longitude: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-longitude') ?? undefined,
    timezone: headers.get('x-vercel-ip-timezone') ?? undefined,
  }
}

export function geo(req: { headers: Headers }): Geo {
  return geoFromHeaders(req.headers)
}

export function isInRegion(geo: Geo, regions: string[]): boolean {
  if (!geo.countryCode) return false
  const code = geo.countryCode.toUpperCase()
  return regions.some((r) => r.toUpperCase() === code)
}

function toRad(deg: number): number {
  return (deg * Math.PI) / 180
}

export function geoDistance(
  lat1: string,
  lon1: string,
  lat2: string,
  lon2: string
): number {
  const R = 6371 // Earth radius in km
  const dLat = toRad(parseFloat(lat2) - parseFloat(lat1))
  const dLon = toRad(parseFloat(lon2) - parseFloat(lon1))
  const a =
    Math.sin(dLat / 2) * Math.sin(dLat / 2) +
    Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat1))) *
      Math.cos(toRad(parseFloat(lat2))) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2) *
      Math.sin(dLon / 2)
  const c = 2 * Math.atan2(Math.sqrt(a), Math.sqrt(1 - a))
  return R * c
}
```

- `??` (nullish coalescing) used so empty-string header values do NOT become `undefined` prematurely (use `null` check, not falsy)
- Actually: `headers.get()` returns `null` if absent; `?? undefined` converts `null` → `undefined` while keeping non-null values, which is correct strict-mode TypeScript for optional fields
- `cf-ipcountry` is the Cloudflare fallback: only used when `x-vercel-ip-country` is absent (null)
- `country` is derived only from `countryCode` via `COUNTRY_NAMES`; returns `undefined` if code not in map

- `geoDistance` implements the standard Haversine formula (R = 6371 km)

All 5 changes are complete. Here's a summary of what was done:

---

### Changes Made

**1. `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.ts`** *(new)*  
Core implementation — no external dependencies, pure TypeScript:
- `Geo` interface with 7 optional fields
- `COUNTRY_NAMES` map (10 entries: AU, BR, CA, CN, DE, FR, GB, IN, JP, US)
- `geoFromHeaders(headers: Headers)` — reads Vercel headers, falls back to `cf-ipcountry` for `countryCode`, derives `country` from the map
- `geo(req: { headers: Headers })` — thin wrapper calling `geoFromHeaders`
- `isInRegion(geo, regions)` — case-insensitive `countryCode` membership check
- `geoDistance(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2)` — Haversine formula, returns km

**2. `packages/next/geo.js`** *(new)*  
CommonJS entry point mirroring `server.js` exactly — object literal + repeated `exports.X` assignments for both CJS and named-import compatibility.

**3. `packages/next/geo.d.ts`** *(new)*  
Type definitions mirroring `server.d.ts` — `export type { Geo }` plus `export { geo, geoFromHeaders, isInRegion, geoDistance }` all from `next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/geo`.

**4. `packages/next/src/server/web/spec-extension/geo.test.ts`** *(new)*  
12 test cases using Jest globals (`describe`/`it`/`expect`), covering:
- Vercel headers → all fields populated + country name lookup
- Missing headers → all `undefined`
- Cloudflare fallback, Vercel precedence over Cloudflare
- Unknown country code → `country: undefined`
- All 6 supported country codes
- `isInRegion`: match, no-match, case-insensitive, missing code, empty list
- `geoDistance`: NY→London (~5,570 km ±50), same point = 0, Paris→Brussels (~265 km)

**5. `packages/next/package.json`** *(modified)*  
Added `"geo.js"` and `"geo.d.ts"` to `files` array after `"form.d.ts"` (alphabetical slot for `g`).
